K_John_Joseph:

I don’t think we play in the same “drinker burg” category. Tough to compare a $95 Bouchard against a Rousseau or a GC Dujac or whatev

my point is more along the lines of - there are better wines for the money.

Chantereves, Berthaut Gerbet, Jean Guyon, Hubert Lignier come to mind. You won’t get the vaunted “terroir” but the wines will be better.

posts.cooked: Curious to hear your thoughts on these producers, Charlie. i think 2019 Bouchard Corton which was 150 or so for ‘normal’ buyers in the US (ie buying retail). Which wines of these other producers would you put ahead at a similar price point? Berthaut Gerbet are very hard to buy (ex Fix-in), Arvelets is $100 or so. Guyon prices have doubled and even buying size 2018/19 doesn’t even give you the chance of buying 20s at the new price. Curious which Ligier 1er/village are cheaper/better than Bouchard Le Corton?

Shan_A:

2019 Bouchard Corton

That’s probably the last Bouchard red I would pick. I buy a lot of Bouchard, but honestly I buy zero Corton. You can still buy 2016 Beze for the $200 range, which is the same price it was going for 3 years ago and half the price of the current releases, which is crazy.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Shan_A:

Berthaut Gerbet are very hard to buy (ex Fix-in)

lots of Gerbert vosne/Gevrey/chambolle on wine-searcher for 19.

Shan_A:

Curious which Ligier 1er/village are cheaper/better than Bouchard Le Corton?

I don’t think Bouchard Corton is beyond more than a decent wine. Let alone for $150. Unless they did something completely exceptional in 2019 outside of their ordinary style, I think you’re better off spending less and buying village wines from the above producers.

I see 2019 Lignier les Chafot for 169, rather have that than Bouchard Corton for 150. They also make a bunch of 1er MSD (Chenevrey, Baulet, Blanchards, Riotte) that I find way more compelling and are around $110-130.

We haven’t even spoken on Chantereves, everything they make is sub $150 for their rouge.

2019 Guyon I can only go off the price I paid and outside of the Echezeaux, everything else was sub $150

posts.name: Greg Kahn

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
Only had one at the lower level and thought it was pretty good for the money.

  • 2019 Bouchard Père et Fils Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru Les Lavières - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru (6/6/2022)
    This pours light ruby in the glass with pink towards the rim. The nose opens with intensely concentrated red fruit: strawberry, candied cherry, pomegranite, crushed rocks, lightly herbaceous bay leaf, cardamom, rose petals, tangerine, and a light touch of cherry liquor. The palate is very concentrated with loads of rich red fruit on entry, medium tannin and medium acid, but the structure really plays second fiddle to the fruit here. The finish shows the rich fruit balanced against light whisps of spice. The finish is quite long with lingering gobs of fruit. All in all this is quite a wonderful bottle of wine, very driven by its large scaled and open fruit. On the other hand the high alcohol and fruit do give this a bit of liquor note that's not my preferred concept of Red Burgundy, so we'll have to see how age treats this. (92 points)

Overall I’m enjoying the 2019 vintage and have found it fairly consistent across various producers. Gobs of wide open fruit on the nose and palate. Alcohols can be a little high. For some this might be a little overdone.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Greg_K:

I think some of the negative comments here are being made on the basis of older experiences. Frederic Weber is making really good reds at the moment.

He’s been the head for six vintages now. I’ve had a wine assortment of their reds since he took over in 2014. Everyone is making decent wines at a minimum now thanks to global warming and better tech/vineyard practices. Doesn’t change the fact there are other producers I’d rather buy at the price point.

You buying 2019 Bouchard corton at $150? Send receipts

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: I’d rather buy Drouhin, Faiveley, or Jadot reds.

A lot of Corton should be 1er Cru instead of Grand Cru. That might make sense of why to choose something else over the Corton. I don’t have any particular insight on Bouchard’s Corton holdings, but that’s my general thought unless someone can tell me otherwise.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: Bouchard’s Corton is Le Corton, and a domaine wine, so neither the terroir nor the winegrowing is subpar. Like Jadot’s Pougets, it’s one of the smarter buys for the money in the range. That said, Bouchard is not a producer I’d cellar and forget without tasting. The oak can be too aggressive for the wine (or the wine-drinker) sometimes. I’ve had stellar bottles from them and in other years disappointments from the same crus. The only 2019 red I’ve had from Bouchard so far is the Jesus, which was good-not-great, but I’ve grown convinced all the Beaune negociants oversell their flagship Beaunes.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
I bought a lot of '10 Cortons, Bouchard’s Le Corton, Jadot’s Pougets and Faiveley’s CdC and they are very different wines. CdC is my favorite, so smooth and elegant, Pougets is very pretty, bright cherry fruit and so easy to drink, Le Corton is a bit darker, lot’s of spice, iron and minerality. I had half bottles of '10/'11 Le Corton last night and they both drank fine. Like all things Burgundy, the price has increased over the years, CdC >$200, Le Corton $150, but Pougets is the value buy today, Zachy’s has the '19 for $104 at the moment.

IMG_24561920×2560 392 KB

I don’t think Bouchard Corton is beyond more than a decent wine. Let alone for $150.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
Bouchard Le Corton was one of my initial wines on the road to Burgundy obsession, and I still have a lot of affection for it. I will admit to having paid $150 once for a back vintage 2009, although I agree that’s excessive. The 2005 vintage is fantastic and I also love the 2009. In general and especially in those two vintages, this is a very robust, rich, black-fruited wine so may not ring your bell if you go for subtlety – in fact I think I got into them because they demonstrated to me that Burgundy wasn’t all fallen leaves and faint notes of cherry. I’ve also found them to be accessible earlier than other Cortons. But they don’t lack complexity – they can have a lot of sweet spice notes (e.g. cinnamon), herbs, and a gamy earthy background that adds interest. You definitely aren’t in California. When I started getting these they were about $75 each and I thought that was a very solid deal for the quality.

I haven’t been as impressed by vintages outside the 2005 & 2009 but haven’t sampled widely (I have a lot of those two).

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: Interesting news on the Jadot Pougets. I had the 2010 like three or four years ago and it definitely wasn’t easy to drink, it was locked down tight and felt like it wouldn’t come out for a decade.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: I drank through a case of the '10 in the 2015-2019 period and the earlier bottles were singing. I used to be in the camp that '10 is going to drink great young and never shut down, but even the '10 Le Corton I had the other night and a recent bottle of '10 Cdc were not drinking as good as they were younger. They all seem less bright, more darker fruits. Looking at the CT notes on Pougets, it seems as if the '05 is the one to drink now. I have had a 6-pack of the '15 so far and while very good I think the '10 is a tad better. I may Coravin a glass from each this weekend and do a comparison. I have not received my '19 Pougets or CdC, but I am expecting to like them a lot - until they eventually shut down.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
I revisited the 16’ Bouchard Le Corton recently and I like it.

Sep 4, 2022: Rich and heady, big savory ripe black fruit balanced by a good spine of acid and grippy tannins. More savory-salty-soy than the strawberry fruit of 14 months ago-seems like its losing some baby fat, and starting to dry out a bit, though this is still a big wine.

Its not a come-to-Jesus-I-hear-the angels-singing level grand cru, but it tastes lovely. Believe it or not these were going for $125 a bottle in Georgia in Spring 21’. Apparently warehouses needed space for new vintages.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I like the reds of Bouchard, Jadot and Drouhin. But, my favorite QPR wines from Corton (relative QPR as this is a Grand Cru Burg) come from Chandon de Briailles. Note, we are talking about Corton with all of these wines and Corton is not a pop and pour wine - it takes lots of time to mature.
personally, I’m spending $100-$150 on Clos des Mouches before I’m spending it on Le Corton if I’m limited to the big houses. or two bottles of Ursules. lol

posts.name: Vlad B3rk0vsky

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Are they more expensive than the Cortons from Bouchard, Jadot, Gaunoux, etc.?

posts.name: Anand Bhagwat
posts.cooked: Yes, 2.5x in some cases. (Jadot Pougets 105-115, CdB CdR 280)

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: True. For Jadot I buy whites and mostly CdN Domaine reds and basically any Beaune red. For Faiveley I like their 1er and GC reds along with the portfolio from Mercurey. For Drouhin, I like the whites and 1er reds, particularly Mouches Rouge and Blanc. From Bouchard the GC whites are good, not a fan of much red.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I think it’s a little ironic that people have decided to pick on the 2019 Corton here, as that is the best young Corton I have ever tasted from Bouchard, and a wine I bought in magnum for my own cellar. And yes, they are doing things differently with it: picking later (it’s from Le Corton, which isn’t the earliest ripening terroir on the hill) and using a lot more whole bunch. I suspect that, on actually tasting it, you may be surprised.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: This thread seems to have turned into a discussion of the Corton in particular, so in an attempt to shift out of that focus - I recently tried the 2019 Bouchard Père & Fils Volnay 1er Cru Les Caillerets Ancienne Cuvée Carnot and found it quite lovely, more serious than I expected, with terrific aromatics and good freshness, energy and persistence despite having recently arrived off the boat. Having paid about $55 before import, I’m am delighted to have the rest of a case.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: Between Bouchard Le Corton and Jadot Corton/Pougets are you buying one, the other, or cellaring both?
